compare.title: Stock Comparison
compare.description: Compare 2-3 stocks side by side to evaluate which offers the best combination of quality, price, and growth potential.
compare.selectAtLeast: Select at least 2 stocks to compare

chart.noData: No price data available for comparison

intro.title: Welcome to Stock Comparison
intro.description: Relative analysis is a cornerstone of value investing. A company's numbers mean little in isolation; they only become meaningful when compared to its competitors. This tool lets you place up to 3 stocks side by side to discover which one truly offers the best combination of quality, price, and growth potential.

selector.title: Select at Least 2 Companies
selector.description: Use this search bar to add stocks you want to compare. For the most meaningful analysis, choose companies from the same sector: comparing a tech firm to a utility would be like comparing apples to oranges. Same-sector comparisons reveal real differences in competitive advantage, efficiency, and management quality. Go ahead. Search and add at least 2 companies to begin!

overview.title: The Big Picture
overview.description: Start here to get a quick snapshot. The composite score combines fundamental and technical factors. Ask yourself: does the category (value, growth, GARP) match your investment strategy? A 'value' stock with a high composite score is a promising starting point for deeper analysis.

fundamentals.title: Financial Health Check
fundamentals.description: This is where value investing truly begins. Green highlights the strongest metric, red the weakest. Key questions: Who has the highest ROE (management efficiency)? The lowest debt (financial safety)? The widest margins (competitive moat)? A high Piotroski score (7-9) signals strong financial health.

valuation.title: Is the Price Right?
valuation.description: Benjamin Graham taught that a great company at a bad price is still a bad investment. Compare each stock's fair value to its current price. The margin of safety tells you how much 'cushion' you have: higher is better. Look for companies that are undervalued AND have strong fundamentals.

radar.title: Visual Profile
radar.description: The radar chart gives you an instant visual comparison. A wider, more balanced shape indicates a company that's strong across multiple dimensions. Be cautious of shapes that are very narrow on one side; it signals a weakness. The ideal value investment shows strength in profitability, solvency, and growth.

price.title: Price vs Value
price.description: Remember: price is what you pay, value is what you get. This chart normalizes all prices to 100 so you can compare performance fairly. A stock that has risen sharply might be overvalued now, while one that has lagged could represent an opportunity. Always cross-reference with the valuation section above.

conclusion.title: Your Analysis Toolkit
conclusion.description: You now have the tools to compare stocks like a value investor. No company is perfect. The goal is to find the best trade-off between quality, price, and growth potential. Use this comparison as a starting point, then dive deeper into each company's detail page for your final evaluation.
